preamble: In English and other Germanic languages, the voiceless alveolar stop /t/ is aspirated [tʰ] in syllable-initial position and unaspirated [t] after /s/ or in unstressed syllables. This is allophony: the two variants are in complementary distribution and never contrast. Native English speakers hear 'tip' [tʰɪp] and 'stick' [stɪk] as containing the same consonant, because the aspiration is phonologically predictable and carries no meaning difference. Note: in many South and East Asian languages (Hindi, Thai, Mandarin), /t/ and /tʰ/ are distinct phonemes forming minimal pairs — a fundamentally different situation from the English allophonic pattern.

examples.note: 'tip' [tʰɪp] vs. 'stick' [stɪk]: word-initial /t/ is aspirated; post-/s/ /t/ is unaspirated. Both are allophones of the single phoneme /t/.
Standard German aspirates /t/ syllable-initially: 'Tag' [tʰaːk] 'day'. Unaspirated in clusters.
Included for contrast: in Thai, [t] and [tʰ] form a phonemic minimal pair — the opposite of the English allophonic pattern.

sources: Ladefoged, P. & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages. Blackwell.
Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics. Harcourt.
Roach, P. (2009). English Phonetics and Phonology. Cambridge University Press.
context: Syllable-initial before stressed vowel = aspirated [tʰ]; post-/s/ = unaspirated [t]
